event: Early Austronesian and Bantu-speaking seafarers begin exploring the western Indian Ocean, foreshadowing the mixed origins of later Comorian settlers.
Bantu-speaking peoples, alongside Austronesian and Arab migrants, establish the first permanent settlements on the Comoro Islands.
The archipelago becomes integrated into the Indian Ocean trade network, exchanging goods between Africa, Arabia, and Madagascar.
Swahili-influenced trading communities develop on the islands, linking the Comoros to the wider Swahili coast.
Islam takes root in the islands as Arab and Shirazi traders establish enduring religious and commercial ties.
Stone mosques and coastal towns flourish, reflecting the islands' growing prosperity from the dhow trade in spices, gold, and slaves.
Sultanates emerge across the islands as local Muslim rulers consolidate power over rival towns.
Shirazi dynasties strengthen their control, dividing the islands into numerous competing sultanates.
Portuguese navigators sight and visit the Comoro Islands during their expansion into the Indian Ocean.
English mariners record visits to the islands, which serve as a provisioning stop for ships rounding Africa.
The islands become a regular watering and supply point for European ships sailing between Europe and the East Indies.
Malagasy raiders from Madagascar repeatedly attack the islands, seizing captives for the slave trade.
Devastating slave raids by Sakalava and Betsimisaraka warriors from Madagascar depopulate parts of the archipelago.
Andriantsoly, a Sakalava ruler, becomes sultan of Mayotte amid continuing upheaval across the islands.
France purchases the island of Mayotte from Sultan Andriantsoly, establishing its first foothold in the archipelago.
France formally takes possession of Mayotte, beginning the colonial era in the Comoros.
Sugar plantations worked by indentured and formerly enslaved laborers expand on Mayotte under French rule.
France establishes protectorates over Grande Comore, Anjouan, and Mohéli, extending its control to the whole archipelago.
The Comoro Islands are administratively attached to the French colony of Madagascar.
France formally annexes the protectorate islands, making the entire archipelago a French colony.
The Comoros becomes a French overseas territory, separated administratively from Madagascar.
Comorians vote to remain a French overseas territory in a referendum under the new French constitution.
France grants the Comoros internal autonomy, with the capital established at Moroni on Grande Comore.
Student protests and growing nationalist sentiment spur demands for independence from France.
A referendum shows overwhelming support for independence on three islands, while Mayotte votes to remain French.
Ahmed Abdallah unilaterally declares the independence of the Comoros, which is admitted to the United Nations as the archipelago's voice.
Mayotte votes in a referendum to remain a French territory, beginning its political separation from the independent Comoros.
Mercenary Bob Denard leads a coup restoring Ahmed Abdallah to power, who proclaims the Federal Islamic Republic of the Comoros.
President Ahmed Abdallah is assassinated, and mercenary commander Bob Denard briefly seizes control before French intervention.
Said Mohamed Djohar is elected president in the country's first multiparty elections.
Bob Denard launches another mercenary coup, prompting a French military operation to expel him.
The islands of Anjouan and Mohéli declare secession from the Comoros, plunging the country into political crisis.
Colonel Azali Assoumani seizes power in a bloodless coup amid the ongoing secession crisis.
A new constitution creates the Union of the Comoros, granting each island greater autonomy to address the secessionist conflict.
Azali Assoumani is elected president of the newly restructured Union of the Comoros.
Ahmed Abdallah Sambi wins the presidency in a peaceful, internationally praised election.
African Union-backed forces invade Anjouan to oust rebel leader Mohamed Bacar and restore central authority.
A referendum extends the presidential term and reduces the powers of the autonomous island governments.
Ikililou Dhoinine is inaugurated as president following the country's rotating-presidency system among the islands.
Mayotte becomes an overseas department of France, deepening its separation from the Union of the Comoros.
Azali Assoumani returns to the presidency through elections, beginning a new period of his rule.
A controversial referendum approves constitutional changes ending the island-rotation of the presidency and allowing extended terms.
Azali Assoumani is re-elected amid opposition claims of fraud and a brief army mutiny in Anjouan.
The Comoros assumes the rotating chairmanship of the African Union, raising its diplomatic profile.
Azali Assoumani wins another disputed presidential term, sparking deadly protests across the islands.
